1.10: Among the circumcised Jews who became Christians.
1.12: Artemas is unknown. ― Tychicus . See Acts of the Apostles ,
1.150: years of age. Callimachus repeated Epimenides' verse in his hymn to Jupiter, and the ancients assert that the Cretans more than deserved the reproach leveled against them by their compatriot Epimenides. Just as living like a Corinthian meant having a sexually dissolute life, behaving like a Cretan meant lying and deceiving: Pierre Dornier, pss.
1.20: . — Saint Paul does not mean that all the works of Christians are pure or good, and all those of unbelievers are impure or bad; but he condemns the doctrine of several Judaizers, some of whom claimed that certain foods were impure by nature; others, that there were meats that Christians should not eat, not because they were impure in themselves, but because they had become so since the Mosaic Law, which forbade them.
1.4: . ― In Nicopolis . There were three Nicopolises: one in Cilicia, a second in Thrace on the Nestus River, and a third in Epirus. It is difficult to determine which one is being referred to here, but the odds are in favor of the last, which was the most important of the three. It had been built by Augustus after the Battle of Actium.

2.1: As for you, speak in accordance with sound doctrine.
2.2: Tell the elderly to be sober, serious, circumspect, sound in faith, in love, in patience.
2.3: Likewise, older women should display holy modesty in their dress, not being gossipy or prone to excesses of wine, but wise counselors,
2.4: capable of teaching young women to love their husbands and children,
2.5: to be kept chaste, occupied with domestic care, good, and submissive each to her husband so that the word of God may not be exposed to any blame.
2.6: Likewise, urge the young men to be wise,
2.7: showing yourself in every respect a model of good works, putting purity and seriousness into your teaching,
2.8: sound and irreproachable speech, in order to confound our adversaries who will have no trouble saying about us.
2.9: To the slaves, he recommends being submissive to their masters, pleasing them in all things, and not contradicting them.,
2.10: not to turn away from anything, but to always show perfect faithfulness, so as to honor in all things the doctrine of God, our Savior.
2.11: For the grace of God has appeared, bringing salvation to all people,
2.12: It teaches us to renounce impiety and worldly desires and to live in the present age with temperance, justice, and godliness.,
2.13: awaiting the blessed hope and glorious appearing of our great God and Savior
2.14: Jesus Christ, who gave himself for us to redeem us from all iniquity and to purify for himself a people that are his very own, zealous for good works.
2.15: This is what you must preach, recommend, and claim with full authority. Let no one despise you.

3.1: Reminds the faithful of their duty to be submissive to magistrates and authorities, to obey them, and to be ready for any good work.,
3.2: to speak ill of no one, to avoid disputes, but to be kind and to show the greatest gentleness towards all men.
3.3: For we too were once foolish, disobedient, led astray, slaves to all kinds of lusts and pleasures, living in malice and envy, hateful and hating one another.
3.4: But when God our Savior revealed his goodness and love for mankind,
3.5: He saved us, not because of righteous things we had done, but because of his mercy, through the washing of rebirth and renewal by the Holy Spirit.
3.6: which he has poured out on us abundantly through Jesus Christ our Savior
3.7: so that, justified by his grace, we might become heirs of eternal life according to our hope.
3.8: This is a trustworthy saying, and I desire that you affirm these things so that those who believe in God may strive to be foremost in doing good works. This is what is good and beneficial for mankind.
3.9: As for foolish questions, genealogies, quarrels, and disputes concerning the Law, avoid them because they are useless and vain.
3.10: As for the one who sows division, after a first and a second warning, remove him from your presence.,
3.11: knowing that such a man is completely perverted and is a sinner condemned by his own judgment.
3.12: When I have sent Artemas or Tychicus to you, hurry to join me in Nicopolis, for I have resolved to spend the winter there.
3.13: Provide carefully for the journey of Zenas, the doctor of the law, and of Apollos, so that they lack nothing.
3.14: Furthermore, let our people also learn to do good works, so as to provide for urgent needs, so that they may not be without fruit.
3.15: All those who are with me greet you; greet those who love us in faith. Grace be with you all. Amen.
